name: Kara Sev
description: Kara Sev is a traditional South Indian spicy snack made from gram flour (besan), rice flour and a blend of spices including crushed black pepper, cumin and garlic. It is crunchy, light and aromatic with a unique flavour that sets it apart from murukku and ribbon pakoda. Kara Sev is a Diwali essential and can be eaten plain or added to South Indian mixture. The garlic and ajwain flavour gets enhanced the day after making.

steps.instruction: Powder 2 tbsp roasted gram (pottukadalai) to a fine powder in a mixer.
Sieve together gram flour, rice flour, fried gram flour and a pinch of baking soda into a large mixing bowl.
Add red chilli powder, ajwain, asafoetida, salt and garlic paste.
Add 1½ tbsp hot oil to the flour mixture. Mix well.
Pour water little by little and make a stiff dough. It should not be sticky or too crumbly.
Sticky dough makes oily kara sev; too stiff and crumbly makes hard kara sev.
steps.tip: The dough should be stiff but smooth — sticky dough makes oily sev; crumbly dough makes hard sev.

steps.instruction: Choose a murukku plate with thick holes for making kara sev.
steps.tip: Use the plate with medium-thick holes — larger holes take longer to fry.

steps.instruction: Grease the murukku mould and transfer half the dough into it.
Heat oil in a kadai on medium heat. Test by dropping a small piece of dough — it should rise without browning quickly.
steps.tip: Fry on medium heat — high heat will brown the outside while leaving the inside raw.

steps.instruction: Hold the murukku maker 2 to 3 inches above the oil surface. Press gently and squeeze the sev across the pan.
When the sev is about 3 inches long, use a spoon to gently break it. The light dough breaks easily and falls into the oil.
Do not add too much at once — overcrowding reduces oil temperature and makes the sev absorb oil.
Fry on medium heat until golden and crisp, stirring often.
Drain on a steel colander or kitchen tissues.
Cool kara sev completely before transferring to an airtight steel or glass jar.
steps.tip: The garlic flavour gets enhanced the next day, so kara sev tastes even better after resting.

tips: The dough should be stiff but smooth — sticky dough makes oily sev; crumbly dough makes hard sev.
Adding fried gram flour gives kara sev a light, airy texture.
Only a pinch of baking soda is needed — too much will make the sev puffy and oily.
Use the plate with medium-thick holes — larger holes take longer to fry.
Fry on medium heat — high heat will brown the outside while leaving the inside raw.
The garlic flavour gets enhanced the next day, so kara sev tastes even better after resting.
For more crunch, increase rice flour up to 5 tbsp.
Kara sev stays crisp for about 2 weeks in an airtight container.
